one_liner: A 58-year-old female presents with episodic flushing, secretory diarrhea, and wheezing, found to have a liver mass and elevated urinary 5-HIAA.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient describes 8 months of episodic flushing of the face and upper chest, occurring several times daily, each episode lasting 1-5 minutes. The flushing is triggered by alcohol, spicy food, emotional stress, and exercise. She has profuse watery diarrhea with 5-8 loose stools per day, unrelated to meals, and a 6 kg weight loss. She has developed episodic wheezing unresponsive to inhaled bronchodilators. She reports intermittent right-sided abdominal discomfort and exertional dyspnea. She was evaluated by a dermatologist for the flushing and by a pulmonologist for the wheezing without a definitive diagnosis. She denies hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, or palpitations.
stage2.pmh: Hypertension, mild intermittent asthma (recently diagnosed but not improving with treatment)
stage2.medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily, albuterol inhaler PRN
stage2.family_history: No family history of neuroendocrine tumors. Father had colon cancer at age 72.
stage2.social_history: Non-smoker. Occasional wine with dinner (triggers flushing). Retired banker. No illicit drug use.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Thin female, diffuse erythematous flushing of the face and neck noted during the examination. HEENT: Facial telangiectasias, no oral lesions. Cardiovascular: Regular rhythm, grade 2/6 systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border (new), possible right-sided S3. Pulmonary: Scattered expiratory wheezes bilaterally. Abdomen: Firm, palpable nodular hepatomegaly (liver palpable 5 cm below costal margin), no ascites, no splenomegaly. Skin: Episodic violaceous flush over face and chest, pellagra-like dermatitis on sun-exposed areas. Extremities: Mild bilateral ankle edema.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated 5-HIAA is highly specific for this condition — produced by serotonin metabolism in neuroendocrine tumors
Elevated general neuroendocrine tumor marker; correlates with tumor burden
Markedly elevated serotonin produced by the neuroendocrine tumor
Mild anemia from chronic disease or nutritional deficiency (niacin/tryptophan depletion)
Hypokalemia from chronic diarrhea; mild hyperglycemia (serotonin can cause glucose intolerance)
Elevated, suggesting right-sided heart failure from carcinoid heart disease
Tryptophan is shunted to serotonin production, depleting niacin synthesis, causing pellagra-like symptoms

imaging_results.result: Multiple hypervascular hepatic metastases (largest 4.5 cm in the right lobe). A 2.8 cm enhancing mass in the distal ileum with mesenteric desmoplastic reaction and calcification. Mesenteric lymphadenopathy. No bowel obstruction.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Primary ileal neuroendocrine tumor with hepatic metastases. The mesenteric desmoplastic reaction is characteristic and can cause mesenteric fibrosis/ischemia. this condition occurs because hepatic metastases release serotonin directly into systemic circulation, bypassing hepatic first-pass metabolism.

imaging_results.result: Intense uptake in the distal ileal mass, multiple hepatic lesions, and mesenteric lymph nodes. No additional sites of disease identified.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Somatostatin receptor imaging confirms neuroendocrine tumor with receptor expression — essential for staging and determining eligibility for peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT).

imaging_results.result: Thickened, retracted tricuspid valve leaflets with moderate-to-severe tricuspid regurgitation. Mild pulmonic stenosis with valve thickening. Right ventricular dilation with reduced systolic function. Normal left-sided valves.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Carcinoid heart disease: serotonin-mediated fibrotic endocardial plaques preferentially affect the right-sided valves (tricuspid and pulmonic), causing regurgitation and stenosis.

stage3.key_tests_rationale: 24-hour urine 5-HIAA is the most specific biochemical test for carcinoid syndrome (~95% specificity when properly collected). Chromogranin A is a sensitive but less specific marker that correlates with tumor burden. CT localizes the primary tumor and metastases. 68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT has superior sensitivity for neuroendocrine tumors and determines somatostatin receptor status. Echocardiogram screens for carcinoid heart disease, which develops in 20-50% of carcinoid syndrome patients.

stage5.final_clue: A patient with the classic this condition triad (flushing, secretory diarrhea, bronchoconstriction), pellagra-like dermatitis from niacin deficiency, new right-sided cardiac murmur (carcinoid heart disease), markedly elevated 24-hour urine 5-HIAA (85 mg), and imaging showing an ileal primary tumor with hepatic metastases confirms the suspected diagnosis secondary to a metastatic small bowel neuroendocrine tumor (NET). The syndrome manifests ONLY after hepatic metastases allow serotonin to bypass hepatic first-pass metabolism.

debrief.classic_presentation: Carcinoid syndrome occurs in ~10% of patients with neuroendocrine tumors (NETs), almost exclusively when hepatic metastases are present (allowing serotonin to bypass hepatic metabolism). The classic triad is episodic flushing (most common, 85%), secretory diarrhea (80%), and bronchoconstriction (15%). Carcinoid heart disease (right-sided valvular fibrosis) develops in 20-50%. Pellagra (dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia) results from tryptophan shunting to serotonin synthesis. The most common primary site is the small bowel (ileum).
debrief.attending_mode_notes: Carcinoid syndrome has several important clinical nuances: (1) The syndrome almost NEVER occurs without hepatic metastases (exceptions: bronchial or ovarian carcinoids that drain directly into systemic circulation) — if carcinoid syndrome is present, assume metastatic disease; (2) 5-HIAA collection requires dietary avoidance of serotonin-rich foods (bananas, avocados, walnuts, tomatoes, pineapple) for 48-72 hours before and during collection to avoid false positives; PPI use can also cause false elevation; (3) Carcinoid crisis (life-threatening hypotension, bronchospasm, flushing) can be precipitated by anesthesia, surgery, biopsy, or chemotherapy — octreotide bolus (500-1000 mcg IV) must be available during any procedure; (4) 68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT has largely replaced Octreoscan with sensitivity >95% vs ~60-80% for Octreoscan; (5) Carcinoid heart disease screening with echocardiography and NT-proBNP should be performed in all carcinoid syndrome patients — valve replacement may be needed; (6) Ki-67 index on biopsy determines tumor grade (G1 <3%, G2 3-20%, G3 >20%) and guides treatment decisions.
debrief.pathophysiology: Neuroendocrine tumors arise from enterochromaffin cells and produce serotonin (5-HT) and other vasoactive substances (histamine, tachykinins, prostaglandins). Normally, the liver metabolizes serotonin via monoamine oxidase to 5-HIAA before it reaches the systemic circulation. When hepatic metastases are present, serotonin is released directly into the systemic circulation, causing the carcinoid syndrome. Serotonin causes diarrhea (intestinal hypermotility via 5-HT3 receptors), while tachykinins and histamine mediate flushing. Chronic serotonin exposure induces fibrotic endocardial plaques on the right-sided heart valves (serotonin stimulates fibroblast proliferation via 5-HT2B receptors), leading to tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonic stenosis. Left-sided valves are protected because pulmonary MAO metabolizes serotonin.
debrief.treatment_overview: Somatostatin analogues (octreotide LAR 20-30 mg IM monthly, lanreotide 120 mg SQ monthly) are first-line for symptom control and tumor growth inhibition. Surgical resection of primary tumor and debulking of hepatic metastases if feasible. Hepatic-directed therapies: transarterial embolization (TAE), chemoembolization (TACE), or radioembolization for liver-dominant disease. Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT) with 177Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera) for progressive disease with positive somatostatin receptor expression (NETTER-1 trial). Telotristat ethyl (tryptophan hydroxylase inhibitor) for carcinoid syndrome diarrhea refractory to SSAs. Niacin supplementation for pellagra. Carcinoid heart disease: valve replacement surgery when hemodynamically significant. Avoid carcinoid crisis triggers; pre-treat with octreotide for any procedure.
high_yield_pearls: Carcinoid syndrome almost always indicates hepatic metastases — serotonin from gut NETs is normally metabolized by the liver before reaching systemic circulation.
5-HIAA collection requires avoidance of serotonin-rich foods (bananas, avocados, walnuts, tomatoes) for 48-72 hours to prevent false positives.
Carcinoid heart disease affects right-sided valves (tricuspid > pulmonic) because pulmonary MAO metabolizes serotonin before it reaches the left heart.
Carcinoid crisis (severe hypotension, bronchospasm) can be triggered by anesthesia, surgery, or biopsy — always pre-treat with IV octreotide.
68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT has >95% sensitivity for well-differentiated NETs and has replaced Octreoscan as the imaging standard.
Pellagra in carcinoid syndrome results from tryptophan diversion to serotonin synthesis, depleting niacin (vitamin B3) — supplement with niacin.
sources: 2023 NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors
Strosberg J et al., NETTER-1 Phase 3 Trial: 177Lu-DOTATATE for Midgut Neuroendocrine Tumors, NEJM 2017
Halperin DM et al., Carcinoid Syndrome: Updates and Current Therapies, Endocrinology and Metabolism Clinics 2023
